name: Elm
summary: Purely-functional ML-family language for building web frontends. Type-system-relevant lesson is restriction-as-design: refuses to expose imperative effects, FFI, runtime exceptions, or higher-kinded abstraction at all, so that ordinary Hindley-Milner inference and a small total subset of the surface remain enough to type the whole program.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Elm chooses the opposite discipline from Koka: the type-system-relevant lesson is **restriction-as-design** — the language refuses to expose imperative effects, FFI, runtime exceptions, or higher-kinded abstraction at all, so that ordinary Hindley-Milner inference and a small total subset of the surface remain enough to type the whole program.

Elm's task/effect architecture and concurrent-FRP runtime story is treated under `data/async-await#api-coloring-and-propagation` (Elm as the constrained-functional-asynchrony counterexample). The type-system contribution recorded here is purely about the design choice: by restricting the language to a small total subset, the type checker stays simple, error messages stay legible, and beginner-friendliness becomes a defensible property.

sections.title: Restriction as design lesson
sections.content:
Elm and Koka represent opposite responses to the question "how much semantic structure should the type system expose?":

- **Koka**: expose effects as first-class structure; let purity, exceptions, async, divergence, and user-defined operations all be visible to inference.
- **Elm**: hide effects in a runtime-managed `Cmd`/`Sub` API; refuse to expose higher-kinded types at all; keep the type system small enough that error messages remain accessible to learners.

Neither approach is universally right. The lesson is that **a language designer should pick a deliberate point on the expressiveness-vs-simplicity spectrum and commit to it**, rather than half-committing in either direction. Elm's success at production-grade beginner-friendliness depends on the restrictions; relaxing them would forfeit the property.
